coredata.dc:title: A two-step strategy for residue-free chemical conversion of plastic waste to carbon dots: Upscaling and solvent recycling prospects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kommula, Bramhaiah
Gehlot, Vaibhav
Kanwar, Kiran
Sil, Supriya
Banoo, Maqsuma
Swarnkar, Abhishek
Rawat, Bhawna
Kailasam, Kamalakannan
Gautam, Ujjal K.
coredata.dc:description: The conversion of polyethylene (PE) into photo-responsive carbon dots (CDs) has emerged as a promising waste plastic mitigating approach by upcycling it into value-added materials. However, this single-step approach for charring of polyethylene (PE) with concentrated H2SO4 followed by in-situ oxidation into CDs limits the reutilization of H2SO4 due to unavoidable dilution. Therefore, we here introduce a two-step approach, wherein char is produced from PE first, separated, and then oxidized to obtain CDs. This way, we could reuse the acid for up to seven cycles. The CDs obtained thus bear similarity up to the third cycle but gradually change thereafter, exhibiting variations in aromatic and amorphous carbon contents, surface functional moieties, and O2 harvesting capabilities. During reutilization, the charring ability of H2SO4 gradually reduces due to its reaction with PE and becomes ineffective after the 7th cycle. To validate the industry feasibility, we scaled up CD preparation from 1 g to 50 g, and found their properties comparable. Further, we have developed a strategy to purify the residual acid of carbon impurities for downstream use after the 7th cycle. Finally, we utilized the CDs' O2 harvesting and photocatalytic abilities to convert benzyl-alcohols into corresponding aldehydes in ambient air.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plastic remediation
Carbon dots
Sustainable processing
Scale-up production
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Large-scale preparation of CDs and validation of their properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: BK is grateful to the IISER Mohali for providing a postdoctoral fellowship, KK is thankful to UGC, India, for a junior research fellowship, and MB is grateful to CSIR (India) for a senior research fellowship. SS thanks UGC for the senior research fellowship, and AS thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for the national postdoctoral fellowship (NPDF-PDF/2022/00373), India. Support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India, under Grant CRG/2021/001420, is acknowledged. The authors thank Gokaldas Exports Charitable Foundation for a CSR grant for carbon dot research.

BK is grateful to the IISER Mohali for providing a postdoctoral fellowship, KK is thankful to UGC, India, for a junior research fellowship, and MB is grateful to CSIR (India) for a senior research fellowship. SS thanks UGC for the senior research fellowship, and AS thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for the national postdoctoral fellowship (NPDF-PDF/2022/00373), India. Support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India, under Grant CRG/2021/001420, is acknowledged. The authors thank Gokaldas Exports Charitable Foundation for a CSR grant for carbon dot research.The following is the Supplementary data to this article.> Supporting Information: Supporting information is available free of charge at DOI: Experimental details, characterization details, FTIR spectra, XPS spectra, elemental composition and their abundance quantification from XPS spectra, PL spectra, TCSPC data, oxygen harvesting, light-induced hypoxia, and autophagy measurements, 1H NMR spectra of products,acid consumption calculations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration showing reutilization of H2SO4 for the char preparation from polyethylene and then converting char into luminescent CDs. Photographs of (b) char obtained in multiple cycles and (c) aqueous solutions of CDs prepared from char obtained in different cycles using the same acid, (d) plot of the amount of char obtained in different cycles of H2SO4 re-utilization while using 1 g of PE, and (e) quantity of the CDs obtained from different batches of char.

(a) TEM images of as-obtained different CDs: The top inset shows the lattice spacings and the bottom inset displays a histogram of their particle size distribution, (b) FTIR spectra, and (c) deconvoluted C-1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared CDs from different cycles.
13C NMR spectra of different CDs prepared using chars obtained by reutilizing H2SO4 (DMSO‑d 6 solvent peak appears ∼40 ppm) in different cycles.

Optical properties of various CDs: (a) UV–visible absorption spectra, the inset showing the zoomed view of absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 200–240 nm, (b–e) contour plots of PL emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, λ ex, in the 300–500 nm range, (f) bar plot of PL lifetime data of different CDs at λ em of 450 and 520 nm.

(a–d) Temporal variation of TO contents in different CD solutions while purging N2, and subsequently during the spontaneous O2 uptake from the air by the N2 saturated CD solutions, (e) plot displaying the TO and RO contents in various aqueous CD solutions, (f–i) temporal variations in the TO contents of aqueous solutions of various CDs under constant illumination of 400 W Xe lamp for 30 min, followed by spontaneous O2 uptake from the ambient air in the dark. (y-axis standard deviations in all the data are a maximum of ±10 μM).

(a) Photographs showing large-scale production of CDs from PE: (i) 30 g of PE cut into smaller pieces, (ii) 62 g of char obtained after the H2SO4 treatment, (iii) aqueous CD solution obtained by chemical oxidation of char in the second step. (b) FTIR, (c) UV–visible absorption and PL emission spectra, (d) contour plot showing PL emission spectra obtained at different excitation wavelengths (λ ex: 300–500 nm), (e) temporal decrease of TO content in CDb aqueous solution (absorbance: 0.6 at 450 nm) while purging N2, and then TO increase during the subsequent, spontaneous O2 uptake from air by the same N2 saturated CDb solution.

Schematic diagram of acid-mediated plastic charring followed by chemical oxidative fragmentation to obtain CDs: the limitations in the single-step approach and attainable advantages with a two-step approach.
Findings from the 4-methyl benzyl alcohol photooxidation attempts by various CDs and benchmarking TiO2. (400 W ozone-free Xe lamp, catalyst: 15 mg, 4-MBA: 0.5 mmol, acetonitrile: 30 ml).

head.ce:title: A two-step strategy for residue-free chemical conversion of plastic waste to carbon dots: Upscaling and solvent recycling prospects

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali, Mohali, SAS Nagar, Punjab, 140306, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali, Mohali, SAS Nagar, Punjab, 140306, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph University, 36 Langford Road, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560027, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, St. Joseph University, 36 Langford Road, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560027, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector-81, Manauli, Mohali, SAS Nagar, 140306, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Functional Nanomaterials, Institute of Nano Science and Technology (INST), Knowledge City, Sector-81, Manauli, SAS Nagar, 140306, Mohali, Punjab, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conversion of polyethylene (PE) into photo-responsive carbon dots (CDs) has emerged as a promising waste plastic mitigating approach by upcycling it into value-added materials. However, this single-step approach for charring of polyethylene (PE) with concentrated H2SO4 followed by in-situ oxidation into CDs limits the reutilization of H2SO4 due to unavoidable dilution. Therefore, we here introduce a two-step approach, wherein char is produced from PE first, separated, and then oxidized to obtain CDs. This way, we could reuse the acid for up to seven cycles. The CDs obtained thus bear similarity up to the third cycle but gradually change thereafter, exhibiting variations in aromatic and amorphous carbon contents, surface functional moieties, and O2 harvesting capabilities. During reutilization, the charring ability of H2SO4 gradually reduces due to its reaction with PE and becomes ineffective after the 7th cycle. To validate the industry feasibility, we scaled up CD preparation from 1 g to 50 g, and found their properties comparable. Further, we have developed a strategy to purify the residual acid of carbon impurities for downstream use after the 7th cycle. Finally, we utilized the CDs' O2 harvesting and photocatalytic abilities to convert benzyl-alcohols into corresponding aldehydes in ambient air.

ce:section.ce:para: The conversion of non-biodegradable waste plastics into functional carbon materials is a promising strategy to mitigate plastic pollution due to its high economic and environmental benefits, attracting recent research attention [1–4]. Over the past century, synthetic plastics have become an everyday-use material due to their low production costs and durability [5,6]. Such plastics comprise a significant amount (>80 %) of carbon atoms; therefore, transforming them into value-added products to favor pollution reduction and resource recycling is a promising yet less-explored approach [4,6–9]. Amongst a wide range of functional carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) are new fluorescent materials emerging as a potent alternative to classical metal-based semiconductor quantum dots [10–15]. They possess distinctive advantages of convenient synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, adjustable photoluminescence, and wide availability of low-cost precursors. These characteristics render them versatile materials suitable for diverse potential applications, including photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, alternative solar energy harvesting strategies, bio-imaging, etc. [16–18].

Some of us recently demonstrated a facile synthesis strategy for converting waste polyethylene (PE) into luminescent carbon dots (PE-CDs) with 100 % conversion efficiency employing an acid-mediated charring route followed by an oxidative fragmentation. Subsequently, this approach was extended to 10 of the most abundant waste plastics as feedstocks to generate many structurally and property-diversified CDs [19]. Importantly, these CDs exhibit three novel properties unlike the usual CDs or other fluorescent or photocatalytic materials known in the literature: (i) molecular O2 harvesting from the ambient air and acting as reservoir cum diffuser of O2 in solvents, (ii) light-induced hypoxia, i.e., the ability to control O2 harvesting by CDs using light as non-contact control, and (iii) autophagy or self-elimination from a solution when not in use (critical for easy disposal of spent CDs), besides their (iv) photocatalytic hyperactivity for oxidative processes due to O2 harvesting capability [7,19–21].

Additionally, from the perspective of processibility, storage, and transportation, this process has distinctive advantages over competing strategies. Nearly all reported CDs are prepared in an aqueous media to investigate their properties, but little attention has been paid to their extraction as solid powders necessary for easy transportation and storage. Such CDs require special purification processes such as dialysis or column chromatography that are significantly time-consuming, solvent-intensive, and non-scalable [17,22,23]. In the plastic-derived process, on the other hand, not only is the conversion 100 %, but the sizes and properties of the CDs are nearly identical too, and do not require an additional purification process [7,19–21,24]. A small volume of ethyl acetate extracts these CDs, which can be evaporated for recycling, and the CDs are recovered as powders. Overall, this approach is promising due to the numerous benefits of efficiently utilizing waste plastics and the unique application prospects of the end-products, storage & transport, making it attractive for industrial utilization, provided the same is scalable to larger quantities [7,19,24,25].

However, the major challenge with this approach is the inability to recover and reuse the acid, the largest quantity of all the chemicals used (its disposal is also complicated), making production upscaling economically non-viable. The use of H2SO4, as such, is not problematic since it is inexpensive and the most widely used acidic solvent in the industry, but for the unique circumstances of its use in CD synthesis where 1 g of plastic requires ∼110 g of highly concentrated acid [19,21,24]. Further use of KMnO4/H2O2 oxidizer and subsequent addition of sizeable amounts of water before extracting the CDs leaves behind not only the toxic metal impurities in the acid but also significantly dilutes it [20,24]. Consequently, charring plastic by H2SO4 becomes ineffective when its pH falls below 10, and recycling this acid content in subsequent CD synthesis cycles becomes impossible [24].

Herein, we introduce a two-step strategy for the conversion of waste plastic into CDs aimed at facilitating the recycling of H2SO4. In the first step, the charring is carried out as in the single-step process, but then the char is skimmed out of it for use in the second cycle so that the acid can be reused for charring the next batch of plastics. In the second step, oxidative fragmentation of char is induced with a small quantity of acid and residue-free H2O2 to generate the CDs. We avoid using KMnO4 so that cleaning Mn4+ is not necessary, and we collect the clean CD powders after a simple extraction with ethyl acetate. We show that the acid can be reused up to seven cycles and characterize in detail the CDs obtained in each cycle. We then scaled the process from 1 g to 30 g of plastics while simultaneously reducing the acid-to-plastic weight ratio from ∼110 g to ∼20 g. The advantages and potential pitfalls of the two approaches are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Additionally, since these CDs exhibit unique O2 harvesting ability to induce controlled photooxidation reactions in ambient air as efficiently as under pressurized O2 [26,27] and accomplish the use of free-air as facile oxidant despite poor diffusion of O2 in solvents, we successfully employed these CDs for efficient photooxidation of 4-methyl benzyl alcohol (4-MBA) to the corresponding aldehyde. All the CDs conduct these reactions using ambient air with much better efficiency than benchmark TiO2.

In our new multi-cycle plastic conversion process, we kept all experimental conditions, such as charring time, temperature, amount of oxidizing agent, etc., the same to the extent possible to understand the influence of acid recycling on the structures and properties of chars and CDs produced during each cycle (and named them as Char-1/CD1, Char-2/CD2, Char-3/CD3, etc.). In each cycle, 1 g of PE was charred with 60 ml (∼110 g) of 18 M H2SO4 (Fig. 1 a). The charr was collected using a ceramic filtration setup, washed multiple times, and dried for use in the next step (Fig. 1b), where we added a known amount of H2O2 to obtain the CDs. Upon adding 10 ml of H2O2, the char dispersed in 5 ml of 18 M H2SO4 quickly turned into a reddish-yellow solution. Note that adding excess H2O2 oxidizes the entire char and transforms the solution colorless, leaving no CDs. These CDs, after extraction, are highly dispersible in water and other polar organic solvents such as ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, and alcohol without precipitation for months (Fig. 1c). Interestingly, a fraction of the CDs were found to be soluble in nonpolar solvents like chloroform and cyclohexane, which will be discussed later.

Initially, for the first three cycles of acid reutilization, the entire PE mass was converted to char, as confirmed by the Fourier-transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (∼2900 cm−1, Fig. S1 and supporting note 1). However, the char contained increasing PE impurities in the subsequent cycles. In the 7th cycle, the transformation of plastic mass to char is only moderate, leading to just the darkening of the plastic surface after 3 h of heating. At this stage, the charring should improve over time, but we maintained only 3 h of heating in each cycle for uniformity. We measured the char production per gram of plastic and CD yield per gram of char obtained at each cycle (Fig. 1d and e). For this, the obtained char was finely ground and separated by filtering with a 0.2 mm mesh from the unreacted PE mass to the maximum possible extent. The Raman spectra show that as the number of reutilization cycles increases, the defect density grows in the char (Fig. S2). We could not visually identify leftover PE at the end of the initial three cycles, but subsequently, we noticed an increased amount of unreacted PE mass (Fig. 1b). Note that the char weight is significantly higher than the plastic precursor (1g) (despite some losses during filtration, as discussed later) due to the incorporation of –SO3H and other functional groups from oxidation. The char production for the first cycle was ∼2.4 g per ∼1 g of PE. As we kept increasing the cycles, the char yield declined, and only ∼1.2 g was obtained after the 7th cycle. Therefore, the yield of the CDs also decreased proportionally (Fig. 1e & Table S1).

Morphology, Composition, and Structure of CDs: The morphology and crystallinity of the CDs have been confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements. Fig. 2 shows the TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the different CDs. All CDs possess a spherical to quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of ∼5–7 nm and a size distribution of ±2 nm (Fig. 2a: bottom inset; Table S1). The high-resolution TEM images of these CDs revealed that all CDs comprised graphitic regions with fringes containing ∼0.35 nm spacings, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite (Fig. 2a: top inset) [22,28–30]. Importantly, we observed that even though the charring amount and the yield of the CDs decline with acid reutilization cycles, this does not affect the size of CDs and their XRD characteristics, showing a broad diffraction peak at 25.9° relates to the (002) graphite planes (Fig. S3) [28,31–33].

We employed FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) techniques to identify the chemical nature and compositions of the CDs obtained from the various cycles. Fig. 2b displays the similar-looking FTIR spectra of these CDs. Upon careful examination, however, we noticed certain variations in the shapes and intensities of the peaks in the spectra, indicating that several functionalities, including the carbon-carbon and the carbon-oxygen functional groups, are in different quantities.

All CDs show a broad peak in the ∼3100-3700 cm−1 region, related to the –OH functional groups. Low-intense peaks are noticed in the ∼2800-3000 cm−1 region, ascribed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrational modes of the –CH3/-CH2 groups [7,19,29,34]. Intense peaks at ∼1720 and ∼1600 cm−1 are seen in the spectra of all CDs and ascribed respectively to the vibrational modes of carbonyl (CO) and aromatic (CC) groups [28,33,35]. The aromatic (CC) peak intensity decreases as we slowly move from CD1 to CD7, implying that the graphitization dwindles as the H2SO4 re-utilization cycles increase. Besides, the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –SO3 groups are observed at ∼1175 and ∼1051 cm−1, respectively, in all the CDs originating from the H2SO4 attack on the polymer backbone [7,19,24,36]. Peaks at ∼885 cm−1 relate to the C–C ring and –CH2 deformations.

The XPS survey spectra of the CDs are displayed in the supporting information (Fig. S4). All CDs exhibited three diagnostic peaks in ∼531, ∼285, and ∼170 eV, corresponding to O-1s, C-1s, and S-2p orbitals, respectively [32,33,37]. The elemental compositions (atomic% from the XPS survey spectra) of these CDs are shown in Table S2, suggesting that as we increase the number of H2SO4 re-utilization cycles, the carbon amount decreases while the concentration of oxygen functional groups grows. Further, to identify the state of each element in more detail, we performed high-resolution scans of the C-1s, O-1s, and S-2p. The C-1s spectra of all the CDs (corrected to the carboxy peak) can be deconvoluted into five peaks, indicating that the CDs comprise five different carbon functionalities in various proportions (Fig. 2c). A pronounced peak for CD1 at ∼284.4(±0.2) eV corresponds to the CC graphitic (sp 2) carbons. A moderately intense peak was observed at ∼284.7(±0.2) eV, corresponding to the aliphatic C–C carbons (defects or sp 3 hybridized carbons). However, the intensity of the sp 2 peak gradually decreases as we continue to recycle H2SO4. Peaks at ∼285.8(±0.2) eV are related to the C–O groups. The peak at ∼286.6(±0.2) eV corresponds to CO groups, and a medium intense peak at ∼289.3(±0.2) eV is ascribed to O–CO (carboxylic) groups [23,28,38]. The relative percentage abundances of the various carbon-carbon and carbon-oxygen bonds in different CDs are shown in Table S3.

Irrespective of the reference peak position used, one clearly notices that the deconvoluted XPS peak positions and intensities are not similar for different CDs, and, therefore, we assume that the sp 2, sp 3 C, along with those from functional groups comprising the CDs are in distinctly different chemical environments. For example, the energy gap between the highest C peak and –COOH in CD1 is around 4.58 eV and has decreased to ∼4.52, ∼4.39, and ∼4.46 eV in CD3, CD5, and CD7, respectively. Also, the energy gap between sp
3 C and –COOH in CD1 and CD3 is ∼4.33 and ∼4.27 eV, respectively, while that between CD5 and CD7 is ∼4.33 and ∼4.40 eV. On the other hand, the difference between the sp 2 C peak and –COOH in CD1 and CD3 are ∼4.71 eV and ∼4.65 eV, while the same for CD5 and CD7 have increased to ∼4.75 eV and ∼5 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the deconvoluted O-1s and S-2p spectra are given in Fig. S5, and it reveals that the CDs are comprised of different oxygen and sulfate/sulfonate functional groups. Therefore, we have undertaken careful 13C NMR spectroscopy to elucidate the chemical environment surrounding the carbon atoms. Fig. 3 shows the 13C NMR spectra for CD1, CD3, CD5, and CD7, highlighting such differences.

The analysis revealed that the CDs comprised four kinds of carbon peaks, where the peaks in the range of 0–40 ppm (range-I) correspond to the alkyl carbon atoms (sp 3 carbons, C–C), and those in the range of 40–80 ppm (range II) are related to those sp 3 carbons containing specific functional groups, i.e., hydroxyl, ether groups, etc. The 100–150 ppm region relates to the aromatic carbons (sp 2 carbon atoms), and the 150–200 ppm region corresponds to the sp 2 carbon atoms as part of functional groups [7,23,39]. The CDs have different numbers of NMR peaks, as well as distinctive peak positions and corresponding intensities. CD1 and CD3 display a good intense peak corresponding to aromatic carbons attached to functional groups. In contrast, CD5 and CD7 show more amorphous carbon and related functional groups. Ratiometric proportions of the aromatic (sp 2 domain) and amorphous (sp 3 domain) regions differ, indicating the structural distinction in these CDs as anticipated from the XPS and FTIR analysis as a consequence of reducing charring capability of H2SO4 in subsequent cycles.

Optical Properties : We then investigated these CDs' photophysical properties, employing UV–visible absorption, photoluminescence (PL), and time-correlated single photon count (TCSPC) spectroscopy. All CDs show comparable spectra with multiple absorption bands in the 200–500 nm region with an optical absorption onset at 500–525 nm (Fig. 4 a). These multiple absorption bands in the 200–300 nm and 300–500 nm are associated with the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of the aromatic core regions and surface functional groups or those in fluorophore moieties, respectively [23,40,41]. The band at 215 nm is related to the π-π∗ transitions, and as the amorphous/sp 3 carbon amount increases, the band gets blue-shifted to 209 nm (inset of Fig. 4a). At the same time, the relative absorbance of other peaks at ∼300 and ∼350 nm decreases.

The steady-state PL emission properties of the CDs dispersed in water were measured at room temperature, as depicted in the PL contour plots in Fig. 4b–e. CDs are generally known to have excitation wavelength-dependent (λ ex) PL emission properties, attributed to the fact that CDs possess multi-chromophoric centers for light absorption and emission, while some CDs also exhibit excitation wavelength-independent PL emission properties [29,32,35]. The contour plots evidence that with red-shifting of λ ex, the PL emission wavelength (λ em) is also red-shifting, and at the same time, PL intensity gradually increases to a maximum value at first and then decreases. Two pronounced PL emission maxima have been observed in all our CDs, separated by about 70 nm. Initially, maximum λ em is observed at ∼450 nm using an λ ex of ∼340 nm and another one with λ em ∼520 nm at an λ ex of ∼390 nm. Remarkably, we noticed that the PL emission in the 300–380 nm range is λex-dependent, while the emission in the 390–500 nm range is λex-independent, indicating that these CDs are comprised of two emissive domains with distinct behaviors. Due to such variations, we observed that the PL quantum yields (QY, Table S1) did not follow a particular order and changed at different λ ex. At 350 nm excitation, the PL-QY variations followed the order of CD3<CD1<CD7<CD5 but the order changed to CD3<CD5<CD1<CD7 at λ ex of ∼390 nm. Furthermore, to evaluate the lifetimes of the photo-induced excitons in the various CDs, we performed TCSPC measurements at PL λ em of 450 nm and λ em of 520 nm employing a λ ex of ∼375 nm (Fig. 4f & S6). The decay profiles could be fitted with triexponential decay functions. The bar diagram reveals that as the acid reutilization recycle increases, the PL also lifetime increases, possibly due to the rise in the number of amorphous defect sites. But interestingly, the same decreases for the last cycle.

Tuneable oxygen harvesting and hypoxia: Our past studies developed a critical application perspective using PE-derived CDs toward harvesting ambient air as a facile and free oxidant in photocatalytic reactions. When small amounts of CDs (∼0.1 wt%) are dispersed in an aqueous medium, they absorb a large amount of molecular oxygen (total O2 concentration ∼650 μM, nearly double that of pure water) from the air in a reversible manner, amounting to >100 oxygen molecules associated with each CD [19,20,24]. Under light irradiation, these surface-absorbed O2 molecules can be converted into various reactive oxygen species (ROS) to participate in oxidative reactions [42]. Further, we demonstrated that the O2 harvesting ability of these CDs is dependent on their complex structure, i.e., the extensiveness of the conjugated domains, defects, types, and amounts of functional groups, in addition to ratiometric proportions of aromatic with amorphous regions [43–45]. Our analysis revealed that when we reutilize H2SO4 multiple times to convert PE, the structural composition of the CDs is nearly similar up to the third cycle and changes thereafter; hence, we anticipated that these CDs' O2 harvesting and the extent of light-induced hypoxia would be different. Note that as these CDs are hygroscopic and weight measurements can be inaccurate, we carried out the O2 harvesting measurements at a CD concentration leading to an identical optical density of ∼0.6 at ∼450 nm, rather than fixing their weight. It may be noted that the CDs have two kinds of adsorbed O2 molecules, those that can be replaced by N2 and the ones that are tightly bound so that N2 cannot displace them. We estimated both in our current samples.

First, we attempted to remove oxygen from the aqueous CD solutions by purging N2 gas for as long as required until this process could remove no more oxygen (Fig. 5 a–d, black plot). Then, we ceased N2 purging and let oxygen diffuse naturally into the CD solutions from the air. The O2 level gradually rises in the solution, initially fast and then slowly, until reaching the initial total O2 (TO) level (Fig. 5a–d, red plot). The dotted horizontal line above the blue area expresses the O2 level in pure water, and the value is ∼310 μM under ambient air exposure. We found that the CDs from every acid reutilization cycle can extract O2 from the ambient air, as reflected by the TO values above the dotted lines. Upon careful comparison, the optical absorption normalized TO content and O2 diffusion rates differ for the different CDs, leading to anticipation of different photocatalytic oxidation activities. In Fig. 5e, we plot saturated and tightly bound/remnant O2 (RO) levels attained in the CD solutions. Therein, it is evident that acid reutilization results in variations in the TO and RO levels of CD solutions where TO values lie between 635 and 628 μM (close to data standard deviation) for the CDs prepared from the initial three acid reutilization cycles, while the same decreases to ∼580 μM for CD5, and ∼525 μM for CD7. The strong affinity of these CDs towards O2 is further evidenced by the fact that RO values are ∼94, ∼83, ∼77, and ∼48 μM for the CD1, CD3, CD5, and CD7, respectively (Fig. 5e).

We then examined the cycling stability of these CD solutions for the oxygen enrichment ability from the air, as shown in supporting information (Fig. S7). Once the N2 purging has ceased, the oxygen diffuses from the ambient air into the CD solutions to attain initial TO saturation values during multiple repeated cycles, as desirable and suggesting easy O2 replenishment during catalytic oxidation reactions. Considering the structural diversity, we further examined the effect of light on the amount adsorbed of molecular O2 on CDs by exposing their aqueous solution to a 400 W Xe lamp for 30 min. The number of adsorbed O2 molecules decreases with time under light illumination, a phenomenon previously we named as hypoxia, due to their interaction with excitons in CDs. As seen in Fig. 5f–i & S8, the TO content decreased from ∼635 to ∼528 μM for the CD1 but regained the initial value gradually once the light illumination ceased. The behavior is similar for other CDs but with subtle variations in the extent and time. CD3, CD5, and CD7 display TO content changes of 97, 73, and 65 μM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Large-scale preparation of CDs and validation of their properties
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the remarkable optoelectronic properties and vast application prospects of the CDs, it is necessary to explore methods for bulk production, which remain lacking for the plastic conversion route, primarily due to the need for large amounts of H2SO4. This, however, is addressable now by employing this two-step strategy. Here, we attempted to synthesize a large quantity of CD by taking 30 g of PE and just 300 ml of 18 M H2SO4, where the PE to acid ratio is 1:10 g/ml while that in conventional synthesis is 1:60 g/ml. Fig. 6 a shows the PE we used and the corresponding 60 g of char (and subsequently the aqueous solution of CDs) thus obtained. As discussed earlier, the increase in the charred mass is due to functionalization by oxygen-containing groups such as –COOH, –SO3H, etc. Fig. 6b shows the FTIR spectrum of the char, revealing that the C–H stretching and bending peaks at 2900 cm−1 and 700-1450 cm−1, respectively, of the unreacted PE diminished after the acid treatment, and a prominent peak at 1620 cm−1, corresponding to the CC groups, appeared [23,29,33,34]. After oxidation of this char to CD (CDb), several functional groups appeared in the FTIR spectra, resembling those observed during small-scale conversion (Fig. 6b).

Similarly, the absorption spectrum of CDb, seen in Fig. 6c, exhibits multiple bands in the 200–500 nm region corresponding to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions. The PL spectra of the CDs show two emission maxima, ∼450 and ∼520 nm, corresponding to λ ex of 340 and 370 nm, respectively (see Fig. 6c and contour plots in Fig. 6d), while their TCSPC PL decay profiles closely match those of CD1 (Fig. S9). Furthermore, it was found that the aqueous CDb solution showed a TO content of ∼642 μM, and after a prolonged N2 purging, the corresponding RO content was ∼97 μM (Fig. 6e). Overall, the optical details and O2 enrichment properties of these CDb are well matched with the CD1 prepared on a smaller scale despite a six-fold decrease in acid quantity and a 30-fold increase in PE precursor. We highlight that no unreacted PE appeared to have been left behind in this large-scale synthesis.

Photocatalytic oxidation of 4-methyl benzyl alcohol (4-MBA) using CDs: As discussed in the previous sections and our earlier reports, the CDs show a broad light absorption in the UV-500 nm range and enormous O2 up-taking abilities from the ambient air. Therefore, these CDs can be used as potential photocatalysts to carry out controlled oxidative reactions by relegating the need for an external O2 supply [21,24,46,47]. We, thus, attempted to convert 4-methyl benzyl alcohol (4-MBA) to the corresponding benzaldehyde (4-MB) as a model reaction under ambient open-air conditions. The reaction conditions, various parameters, and corresponding efficiencies are depicted in Table 1 . The conversion efficiencies are 80 %, 52 %, 46 %, and 69 % for the CD1, CD5, CD7, and CDb, respectively, with 4 h of light irradiation. In each reaction, a minor product was 4-methyl benzoic acid (maximum ∼15 %). The oxidized product was confirmed by 1H NMR spectroscopy by comparing peaks at 2.34, 7.16, and 7.24 ppm for alcohol, 2.43, 7.32, and 7.78 ppm for aldehyde, and 2.43, 7.27, and 7.98 ppm for acid (Figs. S10–18). In addition, we compared the 4-MBA oxidation using a benchmark commercially available TiO2 (P25) photocatalyst, and the yield was only ∼15.6 % (Fig. S18).

Further, to understand the advantages of using these CDs in catalysis, we employed several reaction conditions, i.e., without the CDs or light, we noticed no product formation, which confirms the role of CDs as a photocatalyst. Usually, large amounts of O2 concentrations are required on the catalyst's surface to perform the utmost activities, and to achieve this, continuous O2 purging is utilized in the reported studies. However, it is not needed in our CDs' case due to their intrinsic O2 enrichment properties. We also checked the conversion efficiencies using an O2-saturated condition and found that the product yields (>50 % by CD1). Also, we found that the O2 molecules on the CDs could not be replaced entirely due to the presence of the tightly bound O2 molecules, even by prolonged N2 gas purging (Fig. 5a); therefore, we expected that the CDs might carry out the transformation partially even in N2 atmospheres. We tested the reaction under N2 purging and obtained considerable (>20 %) product yields. It may be noted that no other catalyst is known to do the same, which specifies that the surface-adsorbed O2 molecules are primarily responsible for the oxidative reaction. Furthermore and interestingly, we carried out the reaction under natural sunlight also, while keeping all the reaction parameters the same, and the product yields are comparable to the Xe light illumination.

Autophagy aspects : Finally, a critical aspect in catalysis for sustainability is the appropriate and easy disposal of used catalysts. Failure to do so may lead to their accumulation in the environment, causing pollution, while their recovery might be expensive [48–50]. In that respect, CDs are potentially cheap, non-toxic, and discardable. We have demonstrated that due to their innate photocatalytic activity and high O2 adsorption from the ambient air, waste-plastic-derived CDs can self-eliminate from the reaction mixture by photodegrading each other over several weeks when no other reactants are available to utilize the excitons or the reactive oxygen species generated by them [7,19,21]. This phenomenon was called autophagy. The CDs, which are prepared from a two-step approach, exhibited relatively faster autophagy rates (4 days for complete removal) compared to the single-step synthesis approach requiring ∼14 days in natural sunlight, whose details are given in the supporting information (Fig. S19; Supporting note 2).

The two-step process is expected to mitigate the critical problem of solvent recycling while producing CDs from waste plastic. The primary differences between the CDs made in a single-step and two-step process lie in their production yield, O2 harvesting abilities, autophagy properties, and reactivities. The tunability of char oxidation is not easy in the single step but somewhat controllable in the two-step process, resulting in achievable CD production yields rising to 50–60 % from ∼10 %. In both methods, the O2 harvesting abilities of CDs are nearly similar, reaching 630–650 μM. The autophagy rates of the CDs are, however, different; for example, taking 4–5 days for the two-step CDs to completely disappear, whereas for single-step CDs, it takes about 14 days. Also, the photocatalytic performance of these different CDs decreases as we increase the reutilization cycles. For example, the conversion of benzyl alcohol to the corresponding aldehyde decreases from >80 % to >45 % under identical conditions.

Since the charring ability relies on the strength of the acid, reaction temperature, and diffusion of acid into the layers of plastics, we kept all the parameters the same during the repeated use of acids for CD synthesis, which led to the production of CDs with slightly different properties. Their yields in each cycle were also different. The only possibility this might have occurred is the changing acid strength as we increase acid reutilization cycles since at least 1 in two carbon atoms in polymer ought to form a C–SO3H bond [24]. To verify this, we calculated the acid strength at different cycles and found that approximately 36 mmol could be needed for 1 g of PE mass conversion to char. So, 250 mmol would be consumed during all seven cycles, and the remaining 0.75 mol (out of 1 mol, 60 ml of 18 M H2SO4) of acid is 60 ml equals its 12.5 M solution. A detailed calculation of acid consumption for each cycle is provided in the supporting information as supporting note 3 and Fig. S20. Thus, as we increase the number of cycles, the acid strength gradually decreases, resulting in incomplete charring and moderate darkening of the PE mass. Besides, another possibility also exists that SO2 and H2O will be generated during the carbonization process, which may further react to form H2SO3. Since it is a dibasic acid, the overall pH of the solution would remain the same. The generation of SO3 under heating conditions and its attack on the PE backbone is crucial for carbonization, which may not be effective under dilute conditions, and that's why we hypothesize that charring ceases after the 7th cycle. Such a situation demands that the leftover acid be used for other purposes for sustainability. However, this was found to be problematic during our experiments because the leftover acid contains dissolved char (much tinier pieces than that can not be removed by filtration), and the acid appears as a black dispersion. Therefore, we attempted to remove the char and could accomplish this by simple addition of H2O2 under heating conditions. When we added ∼5 ml of 30 % H2O2 solution dropwise, the black-colored acid (∼35 ml) quickly transformed into a reddish color and then to a transparent acid solution (Fig. S21), leaving no char or CD impurities behind so that the same could be used in further downstream applications.

PE-derived CD scale-up synthesis is crucial to bridge laboratory innovations to industry applications. Therefore, we attempted to check the feasibility of this innovative two-step approach for the sub-hectogram scale conversion of PE mass into photoactive CDs. After thorough characterization and application validation, we noticed that the large-scale prepared CDs' optoelectronic details, novel properties, and innate photoactivity match the lab-scale synthesized CDs. From this, we reckon that this two-step approach is highly suitable for scale-up.

Finally, CDs usually comprise sp 2 hybridized carbon atoms as a core, embedded in the amorphous (sp 3) carbon atoms with different surface functional groups as a shell [16,40,51]. The intrinsic hydrophobic nature of these CD's carbon core is usually shielded by the abundant hydrophilic surface functional moieties, making them highly dispersible and stable in polar organic and aqueous solvents. Most reported synthetic approaches for preparing CDs are primarily based on the hydrothermal/solvothermal method, with water-soluble carbon precursors [17,18,52]. Due to their high solubility in polar solvents, they have vast application prospects, but only in such polar solvents. However, these CDs lack solubility in common nonpolar organic solvents, which is essential for organic transformations in nonpolar, non-aqueous media and optoelectronic devices that require CD deposition using specific nonpolar solvents. In the current two-step approach, interestingly, we accidently noticed that when we add nonpolar organic solvents such as toluene, xylene, cyclohexane, chloroform, etc., to these CD powders, a fraction of the CDs become dispersible in them and maintain the same color (Fig. S22). The UV–visible absorption spectra show that the CDs in different solvents display different absorption bands, possibly due to solvatochromic or because they are just different fractions. Our future studies will focus on these organic dispersible CDs' detailed structural characteristics, optoelectronics, and possible application prospects.

We have demonstrated a sustainable strategy for waste plastic conversion into photoactive CDs by introducing a two-step strategy. Therein, we first converted plastic into char, followed by filtration to separate it from acid. In the second independent step, the char was oxidized to CDs. Due to unavoidable dilution, the single-step approach of PE conversion to CDs seriously limits the reutilization of H2SO4 in multiple cycles, which limits the feasibility of industry utilization. Conversely, we explored the feasibility of reutilizing acid in multiple PE charring cycles in the current two-step approach. Based on the structural and optical characteristics of the CDs produced in different cycles, we found that they bear similarity up to the 3rd cycle but slightly change afterward, where the CDs exhibited variations in sp 2 and sp 3 carbon content along with surface functionalities. We also noticed that the charring ability of acid reduces with each cycle, which is attributed to the reducing sulfuric acid strength after each cycle, rendering it ineffective after the 7th cycle. In addition, to validate the scale-up feasibility of this approach, we conducted a large-scale preparation of char and CDs up to the sub-hectogram scale and found their properties to be comparable with the 1-g scale production. Thereafter, we developed a strategy to clean the leftover acid after the 7th cycle from impurities so that the clean, dilute acid can be used for downstream applications. Finally, we utilized these CDs as photocatalysts for benzyl alcohol oxidation to their corresponding aldehydes in open-air conditions. We showed that the efficiencies are better than the benchmark catalyst, even though the activity decreases with every cycle, and that O2 purging is unnecessary because of their O2 harvesting properties. We believe that the introduction of this two-step strategy would open up the real prospects of plastic conversion to CDs at an industrial scale with many potential applications.

Bramhaiah Kommula: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Vaibhav Gehlot: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kiran Kanwar: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Supriya Sil: Methodology, Investigation. Maqsuma Banoo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Abhishek Swarnkar: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Bhawna Rawat: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kamalakannan Kailasam: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Ujjal K. Gautam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

SS thanks UGC for the senior research fellowship, and AS thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) for the national postdoctoral fellowship (NPDF-PDF/2022/00373), India. Support from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), India, under Grant CRG/2021/001420, is acknowledged. The authors thank Gokaldas Exports Charitable Foundation for a CSR grant for carbon dot research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Supporting Information: Supporting information is available free of charge at DOI: Experimental details, characterization details, FTIR spectra, XPS spectra, elemental composition and their abundance quantification from XPS spectra, PL spectra, TCSPC data, oxygen harvesting, light-induced hypoxia, and autophagy measurements, 1H NMR spectra of products,acid consumption calculations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2024.119960.
